posts.title: Building a Real-Time Kanban with Socket.io
posts.excerpt: How I implemented WebSocket-based task syncing across multiple browser sessions with minimal overhead.

body.text: Real-time collaboration is one of those features that instantly elevates an application from good to memorable. When a user moves a card on one screen and sees it update on another in real time, it feels like magic. But behind that magic is a carefully orchestrated dance of WebSocket connections, state management, and conflict resolution.
Why Socket.io?

Socket.io was the natural choice for this project. It provides fallback transports (long-polling when WebSockets aren't available), automatic reconnection, and a clean event-based API. More importantly, it offers rooms — a way to group connections so that updates are broadcast only to the relevant clients.
For a Kanban board, each board becomes a room. When a user drags a card from 'In Progress' to 'Done', the server validates the move and broadcasts the new state to every other client in that room.
Architecture Overview

The stack is straightforward: React on the frontend, Express + Socket.io on the backend, and an in-memory store with optional Redis persistence. Each board state is a JSON object stored in memory. On every mutation, the new state is broadcast to all connected clients in the same room.

The key insight is to never trust the client's version of the state. The server is the single source of truth. When a client sends a 'moveCard' event, it sends only the card ID, target column, and new index. The server computes the new board state and broadcasts it back.
Handling Conflicts

With multiple users editing the same board, conflicts are inevitable. I implemented a simple versioning system: each board has a version number that increments on every change. If a client's local version is behind, the server sends the full state instead of applying the partial update.
This optimistic concurrency approach works well for small teams (2-5 users). For larger teams, Operational Transform or CRDTs would be more appropriate.

posts.title: GSAP vs Framer Motion — My Take
posts.excerpt: A practical comparison after shipping production apps with both animation libraries.

body.text: Animation libraries are a deeply personal choice in the frontend world. After using both GSAP and Framer Motion in production, I have a clear picture of when each one shines.
GSAP — Absolute Power

GSAP (GreenSock Animation Platform) is the most powerful animation library I've ever used. It can animate virtually anything — DOM elements, SVG, canvas, WebGL, CSS variables, and even JavaScript objects. The timeline feature lets you sequence complex choreographies with surgical precision.
This portfolio site uses GSAP for all scroll-triggered animations, the 3D tilt on project cards, and the hero entrance sequence. The ScrollTrigger plugin alone is worth the price of admission.
Framer Motion — The React Way

Framer Motion feels like it was designed specifically for React's mental model. Declarative animations via the `animate` prop, layout animations that automatically animate between states, and gesture support (drag, hover, tap) out of the box.
For component-level micro-interactions and page transitions in a React app, Framer Motion is hard to beat. The `AnimatePresence` component makes exit animations trivial.
The Verdict

I use both. GSAP for scroll-driven narratives, complex timelines, and animations that need to run outside React's render cycle. Framer Motion for component interactions, layout animations, and quick prototyping.
They're not competitors — they're complementary tools in different parts of the stack.

posts.title: Designing a PCB-inspired UI
posts.excerpt: How I translated circuit board aesthetics into a cohesive web design system.

body.text: The dark theme with orange accents on this portfolio isn't arbitrary. It's inspired by printed circuit boards — the glowing traces, the dark substrate, the precision of component placement.
The Color Palette

PCBs are typically dark green or black with copper traces. I translated this to a dark background (#0D0D0D) with vibrant orange (#FF4500) traces. Orange was chosen over green because it feels more energetic and modern while retaining the 'circuit' feel.
The subtle grid pattern in the background mimics the PCB substrate, and the accent color is used sparingly — just like copper traces on a board.
Typography as Components

Just as a PCB has through-hole components and surface-mount parts, the typography uses two distinct typefaces: 'Black Ops One' for headings (bold, display, like through-hole components) and 'Syne' for body text (clean, readable, like SMD parts).
The Grid System
The layout takes inspiration from PCB routing. Content flows along logical paths, cards have defined boundaries like IC footprints, and the overall structure feels engineered rather than designed.
The scan-line animations and circuit-pattern backgrounds reinforce the theme without overwhelming the content. The goal was to make the theme feel like a natural backdrop rather than a gimmick.
